name: Straight Dink Foundations
description: Stand at the kitchen line and dink straight ahead to your partner, focusing on a consistent contact point in front of your body. Aim to keep every ball below net height with a soft upward paddle face. Focus on quiet hands and a compact swing path.

name: Dink Rally Survival
description: Play cooperative dink rallies with your partner, counting consecutive dinks without errors. The goal is to reach 20 in a row before resetting. If the ball pops up above the net, reset your count and start over.

name: Dink Height Reader
description: Partner feeds dinks at varying heights. If the ball is below the net, dink it back softly. If the ball pops up above the net tape, practice recognizing it but still dink back with control. Call out 'high' or 'low' before each contact to build awareness.

name: Dink Countdown Challenge
description: Start with 10 points. Lose a point for every dink that hits the net or goes long. Gain a point for every 5 consecutive clean dinks. Try to finish the 5-minute session with more points than you started. Builds focus under simple pressure.

name: Cross-Court Spin Dinks
description: From the kitchen line, hit cross-court dinks with intentional underspin and topspin alternation. Focus on brushing under the ball for slice and rolling over the top for topspin. Each set of 10 should alternate spin types.

name: Skinny Singles Dink Game
description: Play skinny singles using only half the court, starting every point with a dink. Rally continues until someone wins the point. First to 11 points wins. This forces precise placement and consistent kitchen line play in a competitive format.

name: Pattern Dink Sequences
description: Dink in a predetermined pattern: two straight, one cross-court, repeat. After mastering the pattern, your partner randomizes their returns and you must decide whether to continue the pattern or adjust based on ball placement. Builds tactical awareness mid-rally.

name: Dink-Only King of the Court
description: Two players dink competitively, scoring points only on winners or forced errors. No speed-ups allowed. First to 7 wins, loser rotates out. The pressure of elimination forces you to find angles and construct points with soft shots only.

name: Disguised Dink Flicks
description: Practice dinking with a late wrist flick to change direction at the last moment. Set up as if hitting straight, then redirect cross-court with a subtle wrist adjustment. Partner should not be able to read the direction until after contact.

name: Full-Court Dink Wars
description: Play full-court doubles but every rally must start with at least 5 dinks before any speed-up is allowed. After the mandatory dink sequence, play out the point freely. This simulates real game transitions from patience to aggression at the kitchen line.

name: Attackable Ball Recognition
description: Dink rally with your partner, but when you recognize a ball above net height, call 'attack' without actually speeding up. Track your accuracy — did the ball truly warrant an attack? Builds the instinct to identify attackable balls without rushing the decision.

name: Fatigue Dink Marathon
description: After doing 20 jumping jacks, immediately start a dink rally. Score is kept competitively — first to 5 points. Between each game, do another set of jumping jacks. Simulates maintaining touch and precision while fatigued in late tournament matches.

name: Static Third Shot Drop
description: Stand at the baseline and drop-feed balls to yourself, practicing a soft third shot drop into the kitchen. Focus on an open paddle face, lifting motion, and making the ball land in the non-volley zone. No partner needed for this foundational drill.

name: Serve and Drop Rally
description: Serve to your partner, they return, and you hit a third shot drop. If the drop lands in the kitchen, the point continues cooperatively. If it misses, reset. This connects the drop shot to the real serve-return sequence you'll face in games.

name: Drop or Drive Read
description: Partner returns serve at varying depths and speeds. If the return is deep and hard, choose to drive. If the return is shorter and softer, choose to drop. Call your decision before making contact to build the habit of reading the incoming ball early.

name: Drop Shot Target Challenge
description: Place a towel in the kitchen as a target. From the baseline, hit 20 third shot drops and count how many land on or near the towel. Try to beat your previous score each round. Simple scoring pressure builds consistency under mild stress.

name: Progressive Distance Drops
description: Start dropping from the transition zone (mid-court), then step back two feet after every 5 successful drops until you reach the baseline. This builds feel for adjusting power and arc based on distance. Reset to mid-court if you miss three in a row.

name: Drop and Transition Forward
description: Hit a third shot drop from the baseline, then immediately move forward toward the kitchen line. Partner volleys the drop back, and you hit a fifth shot drop from the transition zone. Continue advancing until you reach the kitchen line and can dink.

name: Volley Pressure Drop Response
description: Partner stands at the kitchen line and volleys aggressively. You stand at mid-court and must decide whether to reset with a drop or counter-drive based on the volley's pace and depth. Develops real-time decision-making under incoming pressure.

name: Consecutive Clean Drops
description: From the baseline, hit third shot drops. You must get 5 consecutive drops that land in the kitchen to complete a set. If you miss, restart the count. Complete 3 full sets within the time limit. The consecutive requirement creates mounting pressure with each successful shot.

name: Backhand Topspin Drop
description: From the baseline, practice backhand drops with a slight topspin roll to make the ball dip faster over the net. Focus on brushing up and over the ball with a closed paddle face at contact. This advanced technique makes drops harder to attack.

name: Full Point Drop Emphasis
description: Play full points starting from the serve. The serving team must hit a drop on their third shot — no drives allowed on shot three. After the drop, play the point out freely. Track drop quality and point win percentage to correlate good drops with winning outcomes.

name: Two-Option Transition Play
description: Partner alternates between staying back and rushing the net after the return. You must read their positioning and choose: drop if they're at the line, or drive deep if they're hanging back. Builds advanced court awareness and shot selection under real-time conditions.

name: Tournament Simulation Drops
description: Play a simulated match where you start every game at 8-8. The serving team must execute a quality drop on every third shot. If the drop is attackable, the receiving team gets a free point. Simulates high-pressure late-game dropping when it matters most.

name: Forehand Drive Basics
description: Stand at the baseline and have your partner feed balls at waist height. Drive each ball with a firm forehand, focusing on a level swing path and solid contact out in front of your body. Aim for consistent depth past the service line.

name: Drive and Close Rally
description: From the baseline, drive the ball to your partner at the kitchen line. After driving, take two steps forward. Partner blocks the drive back, and you drive again from a closer position. Continue until you reach the line and transition to dinks.

name: Target Zone Drives
description: Partner stands at the kitchen line in different positions (left, center, right). Drive the ball to the open court away from your partner. Before each shot, scan and identify the open space. Builds the habit of looking before hitting.

name: Wall Drive Consistency
description: Stand 10 feet from a wall and drive the ball against it, keeping a rally going. Count consecutive drives without losing control. Try to beat your personal best each round. The wall returns the ball quickly, building reaction speed under self-imposed pressure.

name: Backhand Drive Development
description: Partner feeds balls to your backhand side from mid-court. Drive each ball with a compact backhand stroke, focusing on keeping the paddle face flat through the contact zone. Alternate between cross-court and down-the-line targets every 5 shots.

name: Third Shot Drive Points
description: Play points where the serving team must drive the third shot instead of dropping. After the drive, play the point out normally. Track how often the drive creates an advantage versus getting countered. Helps develop when to drive versus drop in real games.

name: Drive Placement Selection
description: Partner stands at the net in either a ready position or leaning to one side. Read their body position and drive to the open gap — at their feet if centered, or to the open side if leaning. Develops the habit of targeting weaknesses in real time.

name: Speed Drive Exchange
description: Both players at the baseline engage in a drive-only rally. The ball must stay below shoulder height. First player to make an error or hit the ball out loses the rally. Play best of 7 rallies. The fast pace builds pressure and forces controlled aggression.

name: Heavy Topspin Drive
description: From the baseline, hit drives with exaggerated topspin by brushing up aggressively on the ball. The goal is to make the ball dip sharply after crossing the net, landing deep but with heavy downward action. Focus on a low-to-high swing path with fast paddle speed.

name: Drive-Drop Combination Points
description: Play full points where you must alternate between driving and dropping on consecutive shots from the baseline. First shot is a drive, second is a drop, third is a drive. This builds the ability to change pace within a single rally, mimicking advanced match play.

name: Counter-Drive Recognition
description: Partner drives at you from the baseline. Read the ball's trajectory and decide: if it's chest-high or higher, block reset into the kitchen. If it's waist-high, counter-drive back. If it's at your feet, drop. Three options, one split-second decision each time.

name: Drive Battle Elimination
description: Three players rotate in a king-of-the-hill format. Two drive back and forth; first error loses and that player rotates out. The survivor stays on court. Play for 12 minutes straight. Builds tournament-level consistency under fatigue and competitive pressure.

name: Soft Block Fundamentals
description: Partner feeds medium-pace drives at you while you stand at the kitchen line. Practice absorbing the ball with a soft, quiet paddle — no swing, just present the paddle face and let the ball drop into the kitchen. Focus on a relaxed grip and stable wrist.

name: Block and Reset Rally
description: Partner drives from the baseline while you block from the kitchen line. After each successful block that lands in the kitchen, the rally transitions to cooperative dinking. This connects the defensive reset to the dink game you want to establish.

name: High-Low Block Response
description: Partner alternates between driving at your chest and dinking softly. When they drive, block. When they dink, dink back. The randomness forces you to read the incoming shot speed and adjust your response. Call out 'block' or 'dink' before contact.

name: Survive the Barrage
description: Partner hits 10 consecutive drives at you from mid-court. Your goal is to block all 10 back into play without missing. Track your success rate and try to improve each round. Simple but effective pressure to build defensive confidence under sustained attack.

name: Body Shot Defense
description: Partner intentionally targets drives at your body (hip to shoulder zone). Practice turning sideways and using a compact backhand block to redirect the ball into the kitchen. The key is eliminating panic and using footwork to create space for the paddle.

name: Speed-Up Defense Points
description: Start every point in a dink rally. At any moment, one player speeds up the ball. The other player must defend and reset. If the defender successfully resets back to a dink, they win the point. This directly simulates the most common defensive scenario in real games.

name: Reset or Counter Decision
description: Partner attacks with varying power levels. On hard attacks, practice resetting softly into the kitchen. On weak speed-ups that sit high, counter-attack with a volley. Learn to distinguish between balls that demand defense versus balls that invite offense.

name: Two-on-One Defense Drill
description: Two players attack from the kitchen line while you defend alone from mid-court. Try to sustain the rally for 10 consecutive shots by blocking and resetting. The two attackers vary their shots between drives, speed-ups, and angled put-aways.

name: Lob Defense Recovery
description: Partner lobs over your head while you stand at the kitchen line. Practice the full recovery sequence: turn, sprint back, hit a defensive lob or drop, then recover forward to the line. Focus on quick hip rotation and explosive first step backward.

name: Defensive Reset to Offense
description: Play points where you deliberately start on defense — partner gets two free speed-ups while you block and reset. After your second successful reset, the point goes live. Track how often you can transition from pure defense to winning the point through patience.

name: Multi-Threat Defense Read
description: Partner at the kitchen line chooses between a speed-up, an erne attempt, or a lob — all from the same setup position. You must read subtle cues (paddle angle, weight shift, eye direction) to anticipate and defend each option. Develops elite-level defensive instincts.

name: Relentless Attack Survival
description: Two partners take turns attacking you continuously for 2-minute rounds. You must defend and reset as many balls as possible. Between rounds, rest for 30 seconds. Complete 4 rounds. Simulates defending against sustained offensive pressure while fatigued in late-match scenarios.

name: Volley Punch Basics
description: Stand at the kitchen line and have your partner feed soft, high balls. Practice a compact punch volley — short backswing, firm wrist, contact out in front. Aim for consistent placement to your partner's feet. Focus on a short, punching motion rather than a full swing.

name: Speed-Up from Dink Rally
description: Dink cooperatively with your partner. When you get a ball that sits above net height, speed it up with a controlled volley to their body or feet. After the speed-up, play the point out. This practices recognizing and executing offensive opportunities from real dink rallies.

name: High Ball Recognition
description: Partner feeds a random mix of low dinks and high pops. When the ball comes high (above the net tape), hit an aggressive volley. When it stays low, dink back cooperatively. This builds the instinct to recognize and seize offensive opportunities as they arise.

name: Put-Away Accuracy Challenge
description: Partner feeds 15 high balls from the kitchen line. You must put each one away with a firm volley. Score 1 point for each clean winner. Try to score at least 10 out of 15. The scoring format adds pressure to convert easy balls, just like in a real match.

name: Overhead Smash Technique
description: Partner lobs balls to you at the kitchen line. Practice a controlled overhead smash, contacting the ball at full extension above your head. Focus on snapping down with the wrist and aiming at your partner's feet or the open court. Reset between each smash for proper form.

name: Erne Setup and Execution
description: Practice the erne by having your partner dink cross-court toward the sideline. When the ball travels wide enough, jump or step around the kitchen to hit the ball out of the air before it crosses the net plane. Start slow and increase speed as the timing clicks.

name: Attack Direction Choice
description: Two partners stand at the kitchen line. One is stacked to the forehand side, one to the backhand. When you get an attackable ball, choose which opponent to target based on their positioning and paddle readiness. Hit to the weaker defensive position each time.

name: Speed-Up Scoring Game
description: Play dink rallies where either player can speed up at any time. The player who successfully wins the point off a speed-up (or the subsequent rally) earns a point. First to 7 wins. The competitive format pressures you to time speed-ups well and finish points.

name: Roll Volley Precision
description: At the kitchen line, practice roll volleys by contacting the ball with a low-to-high topspin motion, directing it sharply downward past the net. Aim for specific targets on the court using cones. The topspin roll volley is a high-level offensive weapon for angled winners.

name: Point Construction Sequences
description: Play points where you must construct a three-shot offensive sequence: dink to move your opponent wide, dink to the middle to freeze them, then speed up to the open space. If the sequence breaks down, reset to dinking. Builds structured offensive point construction.

name: Counter-Attack vs. Reset Read
description: Partner speeds up at you with varying quality. When their speed-up is clean and powerful, reset defensively. When their speed-up is weak or poorly placed, counter-attack immediately. The drill trains you to distinguish between genuine threats and attackable speed-ups in real time.

name: Finish the Point Challenge
description: Partner feeds you a high ball to start the point. You must finish the point within 3 shots of your speed-up. If you don't finish within 3, the point goes to your partner. Play to 11. This demands decisive, tournament-level finishing ability under competitive pressure.

name: Court Position Shadows
description: Without a ball, shadow the correct court positions for a full point sequence: serve position, transition zone, kitchen line. Practice the footwork and body positioning at each location. Spend 30 seconds at each position, focusing on ready stance and split step timing.

name: Return and Get to the Line
description: Partner serves, you return deep, and immediately sprint to the kitchen line. Focus on making the return high and deep enough to give yourself time to reach the line. Track how often you arrive at the line before your partner's third shot. This is the most fundamental receiving strategy.

name: Shot Selection Basics
description: Partner feeds balls from various positions on the court. Before each shot, call out whether you will drive, drop, or dink based on your position and the ball height. The verbal commitment forces conscious decision-making rather than reactive hitting.

name: First to the Kitchen Wins
description: Play points starting from the serve. The team that reaches the kitchen line first with both players wins a bonus point, regardless of the rally outcome. This adds strategic pressure to prioritize court positioning over shot power in every point.

name: Stacking Positions Practice
description: With your partner, practice the stacking formation on both the serving and receiving sides. Walk through the movements slowly: server signals, both players shift, serve is hit, and both slide into their preferred forehand positions. Repeat until the movement is automatic.

name: Poach and Cover Drill
description: During dink rallies in doubles, the non-engaged partner practices poaching — stepping over to intercept a cross-court dink with an aggressive volley. After the poach, the original player slides over to cover the vacated space. Builds team coordination.

name: Return of Serve Target Selection
description: Practice returns against a serving team. Choose your target based on the server's position: return deep to the server if they stay back, or return to the weaker player if they rush forward. After each return, explain your reasoning to build tactical awareness.

name: Side-Out Conversion Game
description: Play games where the receiving team gets double points for breaking serve (side-out). This creates pressure on the returning team to capitalize on their positional advantage and forces the serving team to play more strategically. First to 11 wins.

name: Isolation Pattern Drilling
description: In doubles, practice systematically targeting the weaker opponent. Execute a pattern: dink to the strong player to set up, then redirect to the weak player to attack. Repeat the pattern until it becomes second nature. This is a core advanced doubles strategy.

name: Full Point Construction
description: Play full points but you must mentally plan a three-shot sequence before each rally. Before the serve, declare your plan (e.g., 'deep return, close to line, dink to forehand'). After the point, evaluate whether you executed the plan or had to adapt. Builds proactive strategic thinking.

name: Timeout Strategy Simulation
description: Play practice games where you must call a strategic timeout at a critical moment (you choose when). During the timeout, verbalize your observations and adjustments to your partner. After the timeout, execute the adjusted strategy. Builds in-match strategic awareness and communication.

name: Comeback Scenario Play
description: Start every game down 2-8 and play to 11. The trailing team must use smart strategy to stage a comeback — no hero shots, just disciplined point construction. Track comeback win rate over multiple games. Simulates the mental and strategic pressure of high-stakes tournament situations.

name: Deep Serve Placement
description: Practice serving deep to the baseline from the standard serving position. Place a cone one foot inside the baseline and aim to land the ball between the cone and the back line. Focus on consistent underhand motion and follow-through toward the target.

name: Serve and Third Shot Combo
description: Serve deep to your partner, who returns the ball. Focus on connecting your serve with a quality third shot (drop or drive). The goal is to practice the serve not as an isolated skill but as the start of a real point sequence.

name: Serve Target Reading
description: Before each serve, have your partner stand in a ready position on the return side. Read their stance — if they shade to the forehand, serve to their backhand, and vice versa. This builds the habit of observing the returner before committing to serve direction.

name: Serve Accuracy Contest
description: Divide the service box into quadrants using cones. Serve 20 balls, calling your target quadrant before each serve. Score 1 point for hitting the correct quadrant. Try to score 12 or more out of 20. The pre-commitment and scoring adds pressure to each serve.

name: Topspin Serve Development
description: Practice serves with a topspin motion, contacting the ball with a low-to-high paddle swing to create forward rotation. The topspin serve should kick up after bouncing, making the return more difficult. Focus on consistent ball toss and the upward brushing motion.

name: Serve Variation Points
description: Play points where you must use a different serve type each time you serve: first point deep flat, second point with spin, third point short, fourth point wide. After 4 serves, repeat the cycle. Track which serve types lead to the most third-shot advantages.

name: Returner Weakness Exploit
description: Serve against a returner for a full game. After every two points, identify the returner's weakest return position and deliberately target it. Adjust your strategy mid-game as the returner adapts. Develops the ability to probe and exploit serve return weaknesses.

name: Must-Make Serve Streaks
description: Hit 10 serves in a row. If you fault, restart the count. You must complete 10 consecutive legal serves that land deep before you can stop. Track how many attempts it takes. This builds serve reliability under the pressure of consecutive success.

name: Power Serve with Placement
description: Combine maximum paddle speed with precise placement. Serve with full power targeting specific corners of the service box. Use cones as targets and alternate between cross-court and down-the-line power serves. The goal is power without sacrificing accuracy.

name: Serve-to-Setup Strategy
description: Play points with a predefined serve-to-third-shot plan. Before each serve, decide: if you serve deep to the backhand, plan a third shot drop; if you serve wide, plan a third shot drive down the line. Execute the full two-shot strategy and evaluate success.

name: Adaptive Serve Sequencing
description: Play a full game focusing on serve sequencing. Never serve the same type twice in a row. After each game, review which sequences worked best against the returner and adjust for the next game. Develops the ability to keep opponents off-balance with serve variety and sequencing intelligence.

name: Match-Point Serve Simulation
description: Simulate match point serving: stand at the service line, take a deep breath, and serve as if the game depends on it. Partner returns and you play the point out. If you win, you earned 'the match.' If you lose, reset and try again. Must win 5 'match points' to complete the drill.
